tree.heading: 1. SCOPE
tree.content_preview: This guideline is intended for the renal multidisciplinary team (MDT) including consultant nephrologists, specialist registrars, specialist nurses, dietitians and pharmacists. This is a locally adapte

tree.heading: 2. INTRODUCTION
tree.content_preview: All patients should be reviewed within one month of starting HD by a renal dietitian (Renal Association, 2010). All HD patients should have a nutritional review at least every three-six months, and pa

tree.heading: 3. STATEMENT OF INTENT
tree.content_preview:
During the dietary assessment of HD patients the following aspects should be considered:

Energy:

Aim 30-35Kcal /Kg/ IBW/d

(EDTNA, 2002;K/DOQI, 2002)

Protein:

Aim minimum 1.1g /Kg/ IBW/d

(RNG, 20

tree.heading: Fluid: Aim for 500ml + previous days urine output (EDTNA, 2002)
tree.content_preview: Patients should be advised on a maximum weight gain of 2Kg or 4% of dry weight.

tree.heading: Vitamin and mineral intake:
tree.content_preview: The dietitian will advise on ways to achieve an adequate intake of vitamins and minerals.

tree.heading: 4.DEFINITIONS
tree.content_preview: CKD- Chronic Kidney Disease HD- Haemodialysis IBW- Ideal body weight iPTH- Intact Parathyroid hormone MAC- Mid-arm circumference TSF- Tricep skinfold thickness IDWG- Intra-dialytic weight gain JVP - J

tree.heading: 5.DUTIES / RESPONSIBILITIES
tree.content_preview: All renal dietitians should be aware and understand this guideline to assist with its implementation. This will ensure that all CKD HD patients that are referred to a renal dietitian are given dietary

tree.heading: Assessment
tree.content_preview: The prioritisation and frequency of dietetic review will be decided by the patient's renal dietitian (NHS Commissioning Board, 2013). The frequency of monitoring may need to increase to monthly in pat

tree.heading: Aim: Renal Association standards recommend pre-dialysis serum potassium levels are maintained between 4-6 mmol/l
tree.content_preview:
 Where serum potassium levels are elevated non dietary causes of raised potassium should be considered before dietary restriction (Appendix D)
 Aim for a normal range of serum bicarbonate (22-29mmol

children.heading: (iv) Phosphate:
children.content_preview:
Aim: To maintain serum phosphate levels between 1.1-1.7mmol/l (Renal Association, 2010)
 Calcium levels should be maintained at 2.10-2.5 mmol/l (corrected for serum albumin)
 The dose of calcium bas
ancestor_path: Aim: Renal Association standards recommend pre-dialysis serum potassium levels are maintained between 4-6 mmol/l

children.heading: (v) Intact Parathyroid Hormone: 8 - 38 pmol/L (2-9 times the upper laboratory target)
children.content_preview: Treatment should be based on trends; patients with levels of iPTH above the target range should be assessed for hyperphosphataemia and hypocalcaemia (KDIGO, 2009). Active vitamin D analogues calcitrio

tree.heading: Aim: Control of Hypertension Support of fluid management
tree.content_preview:
 Dietary salt intakes >6g/d precipitate hypertension. Aim 100mmol/6g salt a day
 Reducing dietary salt intake and sodium dialysate concentration has been shown to reduce blood pressure (McMahon e

children.heading: (vi) Fluid and interdialytic weight gains (IDWG)
ancestor_path: Aim: Control of Hypertension Support of fluid management

tree.heading: Aim: To help maintain an even fluid balance and control IDWG's
tree.content_preview:
 Patients should be advised on an appropriate level of IDWG. Large fluid weight gains are associated with symptomatic dialysis sessions and left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH)
 Patients should be adv

children.heading: (vii) Dry Weight
children.content_preview:  Changes in dry weight are an important consideration in the review of HD patients. Such changes are often subtle and difficult to identify. The dietitian should be informed if patients are losing dr
ancestor_path: Aim: To help maintain an even fluid balance and control IDWG's

tree.heading: Aim: To improve intake of vitamins and minerals
tree.content_preview:
The dietitian will advise on ways to achieve an adequate intake of vitamins and minerals is adequate.
 Water-soluble vitamins are routinely given to HD patients on the renal unit post dialysis, 2-3 x

tree.heading: Vitamin D
tree.content_preview: NICE (2014) recommend the diagnosis and treatment of native vitamin D deficiency and insufficiency in those with a GFR <30mls/min. This follows on from European guideline recommendations which reco

children.heading: (ix) Dialysis adequacy and changes in regime.
children.content_preview:
 Aim URR >65% for those patients dialysing three times per week (Renal Association, 2009)
 Aim URR >85% for those patients dialysing two times per week. Twice weekly HD is not recommended unle

children.heading: (x) Undernutrition
children.content_preview:
 Nutritional support should be considered where patients are not achieving adequate nutrition (K/DOQI, 2000)
 Dietitians should ensure patients are not following any unnecessary dietary restrictions
ancestor_path: (ix) Dialysis adequacy and changes in regime.

children.heading: (xi) Documentation of dietary review
children.content_preview:  When patients are reviewed outcomes should be discussed with the relevant nurse and documented in the nursing kardex in the nutritional care plan or medical notes for inpatients
ancestor_path: (ix) Dialysis adequacy and changes in regime.

tree.heading: 7. DISSEMINATION AND IMPLEMENTATION
tree.content_preview: These guidelines have been disseminated and implemented through the dietetic and renal QIPPS meetings, and are the e-library so that all members of the renal multi-disciplinary team has assess to them

tree.heading: 8.TRAINING
tree.content_preview: All new renal dietitians have an introduction and training programme. Included within this training is the implementation of the renal dietetic guidelines.

tree.heading: Appendix A
tree.content_preview:
Coventry renal dietitians

Out patient referral criteria for renal dietetic review

tree.heading: Please refer the following patients for dietetic review:
Dialysis patients:
tree.content_preview:
If patients have not been seen by a dietitian since starting dialysis. If blood results are outside of the following and this is not a longstanding problem:

| | HD | PD |
|-----------|---------------

tree.heading: Transplant patients:
tree.content_preview:
Patients have gained unwanted extra weight - BMI >30

Newly diagnosed diabetics

Raised cholesterol - > 5.5mmol/l

tree.heading: Pre-Dialysis patients:
tree.content_preview:
Stage 4-5 CKD, and any of the following:

Phosphate > 1.40mmol/l

Potassium > 5.5mmol/l with a normal bicarbonate

Uraemic symptoms, including poor appetite or weight loss

Any questions about d

Weight (Kg)/

(For HD

tree.heading: Renal Association Guidelines 2010; Screening for undernutrition in CKD
Screening methods for undernutrition in CKD
tree.content_preview:
We recommend that all patients with stage 4-5 CKD should have the following parameters measured as a minimum in order to identify undernutrition (1C):
 Actual Body Weight (ABW) (< 85% of Ideal Bod

tree.heading: Appendix D: Factors affecting serum potassium levels in renal failure
tree.content_preview:
Normal serum potassium levels: 3.5 - 5.5 mmol/l

HD patients: 3.5 - 6.0 mmol/l

PD patients : 3.5 - 5.5 mmol/l

An INCREASE in serum potassium could be due to :
 Diet
o High potassium foods
 Drugs


tree.heading: Treatment for high potassium:
tree.content_preview:
1. Calcium gluconate

2. Dextrose and insulin. This works by moving potassium into the cells; temporary measure

3. Dialysis - haemodialysis, haemofiltration

4. Ion exchanges resins e.g. calcium reso

tree.heading: A DECREASE in serum potassium could be due to:
tree.content_preview:
 Diet
o Over enthusiastic dietary restriction
 Drugs
o Diuretics that cause potassium loss e.g. frusemide (note: potassium levels will rise when patient ceases these)
o Salbutamol - orally or high d
